天文学团队在 2 年前捕捉到了潜伏在梅西耶 87 (M87) 星系中心的巨大黑洞的第一个特写镜头，现在已经放大了附近活动星系半人马座 A 中的第二个稍小的巨星. 事件视界望远镜 (EHT) 的最新图像应该有助于解决这样的问题，即这样的星系中心如何将大量物质聚集成强大的光束并将它们发射到数千光年的太空中。这些图像一起也支持了理论家的信念，即所有黑洞都以相同的方式运作，尽管它们的质量存在巨大差异。 “这真是太好了，”爱丁堡大学的天文学家菲利普·贝斯特谈到新的 EHT 图像时说。 “与这些喷流的先前图像相比，角分辨率令人惊讶。” EHT 将数十个广泛分布的无线电天线（从夏威夷到法国，从格陵兰到南极）合并成一个巨大的虚拟望远镜。通过同时将大量碟形物体指向一个天体，并用原子钟仔细地为每个碟形物体的数据打上时间戳，研究人员随后可以用大规模计算集群重新组装它——这个过程需要数年时间——以生成具有分辨率与单个地球大小的碟子一样清晰。一项挑战是同时获得 11 个不同天文台的观测时间，因此 EHT 每年仅运行几周；恶劣的天气和技术故障通常会进一步缩小该窗口。虚拟望远镜在 2017 年的同一观测活动中探测了半人马 A，该活动产生了现在著名的 M87 超大质量黑洞图像——2019 年科学年度突破。半人马 A 距离大约 1300 万光年，是其中之一。离地球最近的星系，在无线电波长下是明亮的。它也有明显的喷流在银盘上方和下方喷出物质，这是一个活跃的巨型黑洞的标志。马克斯普朗克射电天文学研究所的团队成员迈克尔詹森说：“我们想看看 EHT 可以提供的分辨率下喷流的样子。” “我们不知道会发生什么。”他和他的同事今天在《自然天文学》杂志上报告的结果是喷流如何从半人马座 A 的超大质量黑洞周围区域出现的详细图像，显示出与 EHT 的 M87 喷流图像在更小尺度上的显着相似性。其他望远镜在不同波长拍摄的半人马座 A 喷流图像显示的细节很少，但 EHT 图像显示喷流的中心较暗，两侧有两条明亮的条纹；贝斯特认为，喷流的边缘可能看起来很亮，因为它的外部区域与周围的气体和尘埃摩擦，使它们发光。天体物理学家并不完全了解星系核是如何驱动这些极其强大的喷泉的。一种理论认为，吸积盘，即螺旋进入黑洞的物质漩涡，会产生一个磁场，将一些物质聚集成喷射流。其他人则认为，这个磁场必须利用黑洞本身的旋转能量才能获得如此巨大的能量。对半人马座 A 的新观测并没有解决这个问题，但它们提供了线索。詹森说，图像显示喷流的平行边缘在靠近黑洞的地方变窄成锥形。他说，那个锥体的底部仍然很宽，这可能表明它来自吸积盘。 “还有待观察，”他说。
圣约翰学院的理论天体物理学家吉姆·比尔 (Jim Beall) 表示，可能没有唯一的答案：黑洞的自转会拖曳下落物质最内部的稳定轨道，这反过来又会影响吸积盘的形状和为喷流提供动力的方式。 “这是一种共生关系，”他说。 “EHT 让我们靠近吸积盘。结果真的很漂亮。”半人马座 A 的照片也填补了黑洞观测中的尺寸空白。观察者研究了来自最大黑洞（包括 M87）的喷流的工作原理，该黑洞的质量是太阳质量的数十亿倍。他们还看到了来自小得多的黑洞的喷流，质量有几十个太阳。半人马座 A 的新视图，质量是太阳质量的 5500 万倍，看起来就像它越来越小的亲戚。这证实了黑洞本质上是简单物体的观点，仅由它们的质量、电荷和自旋来定义，因此具有大恒星质量的黑洞的行为应该与具有星系质量的黑洞没有区别。
note_en:
The astronomy team that 2 years ago captured the first close-up of a giant black hole, lurking at the center of the galaxy Messier 87 (M87), has now zoomed in on a second, somewhat smaller giant in the nearby active galaxy Centaurus A. The Event Horizon Telescope’s (EHT’s) latest image should help resolve questions about how such galactic centers funnel huge amounts of matter into powerful beams and fire them thousands of light-years into space. Together the images also support theorists’ belief that all black holes operate the same way, despite huge variations in their masses.
 “This is really nice,” astronomer Philip Best of the University of Edinburgh says of the new EHT image. “The angular resolution is astonishing compared to previous images of these jets.”
 The EHT merges dozens of widely dispersed radio dishes, from Hawaii to France and from Greenland to the South Pole, into a huge virtual telescope. By pointing a large number of dishes at a celestial object at the same time and carefully time stamping the data from each one with an atomic clock, researchers can later reassemble it with massive computing clusters—a process that takes years—to produce an image with a resolution as sharp as that of a single Earth-size dish. One challenge is getting observing time on 11 different observatories simultaneously, so the EHT only operates for a few weeks each year; poor weather and technical glitches often further narrow that window.
 The virtual telescope probed Centaurus A during the same 2017 observing campaign that produced the  now-famous image of the supermassive black hole in M87— Science’s  Breakthrough of the Year for 2019. Centaurus A, about 13 million light-years away, is one of the closest galaxies to Earth that is bright at radio wavelengths. It also has obvious jets spewing matter above and below the galactic disk, a hallmark of an active giant black hole. “We wanted to see what the jet looked like at the resolution” EHT could offer, says team member Michael Janssen of the Max Planck Institute for Radio Astronomy. “We didn’t know what to expect.”
    The result, which he and colleagues report today in  Nature Astronomy, was a  detailed image of how the jet emerges from the region around Centaurus A’s supermassive black hole, showing a remarkable similarity to EHT’s images of M87’s jet on a much smaller scale. Images of Centaurus A’s jets taken by other telescopes at different wavelengths revealed little detail, but the EHT images show the jet with a dark center flanked by two bright stripes; Best suggests the jet may appear bright around its edge because its outer regions rub against surrounding gas and dust, causing them to glow.
 Astrophysicists don’t fully understand how galactic nuclei drive these fantastically powerful fountains. One theory holds that an accretion disk, the swirling whirlpool of matter spiraling into the black hole, generates a magnetic field that funnels some of the matter into a jet. Others argue this magnetic field must tap into the rotational energy of the black hole itself to be able to achieve such colossal power.
 The new observations of Centaurus A don’t resolve that question, but they hold clues. Janssen says the images show that the remarkably parallel edges of the jet narrow into a cone close to the black hole. The base of that cone remains wide, he says, which might suggest it is coming from the accretion disk. “It remains to be seen,” he says.
 Theoretical astrophysicist Jim Beall of St. John’s College says there may be no single answer: The spin of the black hole drags on the innermost stable orbit of the infalling matter, which in turn affects how the accretion disk shapes and powers the jet. “It’s a symbiotic relationship,” he says. “The EHT takes us down close to the accretion disk. The results are really quite beautiful.”
 The pictures of Centaurus A also fill in a size gap in black hole observations. Observers have studied the workings of  jets coming from the very largest of black holes—including M87—weighing billions of times the mass of our Sun. They’ve also seen jets from much smaller black holes, with masses of a few tens of Suns. The new view of Centaurus A, at 55 million times the Sun’s mass, looks just like its bigger and smaller relatives. This confirms the idea that black holes are essentially simple objects, defined only by their mass, charge, and spin, so those with the mass of a large star should behave no differently from one with the mass of a galaxy.
title: 事件视界望远镜捕捉到第二个黑洞喷流的“美丽”图像
title_en: Event Horizon Telescope captures ‘beautiful’ images of second black hole’s jet

note:
我正在考虑成为一名外籍人士（“外籍人士”）——至少在国外生活一段时间。问题是，在哪里？我已经做了一些工作来弄清楚哪些地方最实惠；现在我想知道在哪里可以找到最好的天气。 （注：在写这篇文章的过程中，我得出的结论是，天气只是影响我计划的一个因素，不一定是最重要的。因此，我没有完全完成最初预期的天气探索。因此，这种分析的最佳用途可能是在考虑特定地点的天气时确定感兴趣的标准。在一些地方，这篇不完整的文章也可能存在连贯性的小问题。）在试图找到世界上最好的天气时，有一些我必须理清的事情。一是你可以拥有一切。寒冷的冬天可以有四个季节，也可以有美好、温暖、阳光明媚的一月天气。你不能把它放在同一个地方。你必须上车开车，或者爬上飞机飞。为了享受这种灵活性，生活在一个地理和气候多样化的国家是有帮助的，比如美国——或者（从谷歌地球来看）阿根廷，或者意大利；或者在山脉从海中拔地而起的地方，将这些寒冷和温暖的地方放在一起，例如南美洲的西海岸。但我并不打算成为一只雪鸟——在温暖的地方度过冬天的几个月，也就是说，然后在夏季的几个月里去其他地方。虽然我不确定这项研究说了什么，但我听说一年中大部分时间都离开的人可能会发现自己主要与其他雪鸟交往，如果有的话并且对社交感兴趣的话；他们可能不会非常深入地参与他们居住地的社会和社区。对于那些想要在目标国家获得居民签证优势的人来说，雪鸟之旅也可能很困难：显然，至少在最初几年，可能会有法律限制可以离开多长时间。当然，在不同地点之间来回穿梭也会产生差旅费，以及维护两处房产、将全套财产存放在两个地方和/或仅限于短期租赁的更高成本。这里的任务是确定一个我想留下的目的地。并不是说我不会考虑去其他地方；我只是想让我的第一个选择成为最好的。这提出了一个问题：在天气中，什么是“最好的”？我认为这个问题有一个很好的答案——这不一定只是一个意见问题——而且它很重要。 Lucas 和 Lawless（2013 年，被 Darack 误解，2014 年；accord Feddersen 等人，2013 年）发现天气对普通人的生活满意度没有影响。但这可能不适用于将日常户外活动作为首选生活方式的重要因素，或者社交互动受天气条件影响的人（例如，Perry，2014 年）——尤其是雪鸟（Simpson & Siguaw，2012 年）。大量研究表明，暴露于户外往往会对心理健康产生积极影响（例如，Bratman 等，2015；Buchecker & Degenhardt，2015；Whear 等，2014；另见 Dolan & Laffan，2016 ）。因此，如果好天气让一个人更频繁地外出，那么追求好天气可能会有所帮助。沿着这些思路，搜索导致了另外几篇有趣的文章：在对中国办公室工作人员的一项研究中，与其他研究一致，Cai (2013) 发现工作效率和对工作站位置的满意度通常随着靠近窗户而增加（但坐在靠近窗户的办公桌前的人下降）并且建筑物的东侧、南侧和西侧（即至少部分阳光充足）高于北侧。
Glimcher 和 Tymula（2015 年）引用了研究发现，老年人更容易受到天气和气候变化的影响；当前几天不阴天时，人们在阴天时更厌恶风险；亮度（即阳光的强度和持续时间）促进了乐观但也促进了不一致，尤其是当亮度高于前几天时；坏情绪（即悲观主义）提高了分析性能和记忆力（表明提高了一致性）。 Chang (c. 2016) 暗示（但没有具体引用）研究表明，在户外度过的时间可以通过多种方式改善健康：减轻压力和抑郁水平；提高创造力和生产力；提高集中注意力的能力；维生素 D 吸收；对于老年人，改善活动能力和参与日常活动的能力，减少对疼痛和睡眠问题的抱怨；提高老年痴呆症和中风患者的社交技能；增强免疫系统；并改善眼睛健康、睡眠质量和自尊。 ASC (nd) 回应了这些观点，并补充说（引用）室内空气往往比室外空气污染严重得多，并且户外活动通常会增加人们（即不仅仅是老年人）的流动性、能量水平和幸福感。简而言之，这些消息来源表明，正如人们所料，暴露于户外——尤其是户外活动——对身心健康有益。因此，我决定最好的天气是最能鼓励人们去户外活动并留在户外的天气。为此，关键的天气因素将是阳光、温暖和干燥——让我们想起 Brian Brettschneider 提供的“沉闷天气指数”：来自边界南部的移民可能是德克萨斯州、亚利桑那州城市人口显着增长的部分原因、佛罗里达州和南加州。但这并没有解释从密尔沃基到罗切斯特的锈带城市的人口下降。至少一个世纪以来，美国人一直涌向南部和西南部各州的退休目的地。虽然一些内部移民可能会考虑税收减免，但没有可比的流向南达科他州或北部其他低税收州（例如，参见 Conway & Rork，2016 年，第 1012、1021 页；Mazerov，2014 年）。同样，从我在浏览中看到的情况来看，外籍人士退休的首选国家往往更接近赤道：例如墨西哥和泰国——大多数情况下不是加拿大或瑞典，尽管它们具有潜在的社会、文化和/或语言优势。愿意冒险的美国退休人员似乎更倾向于考虑负担得起的葡萄牙或巴拿马，而不是负担得起的爱沙尼亚或西伯利亚。当然，并不是每个人都会享受相同的气候或相同的天气事件。其中一些是由于个人的身体特征：新陈代谢和一个人身体的其他方面会影响他/她对较暖或较冷温度的反应。有些是由于人们遇到天气的条件。对在阳光下做体力劳动的人来说是温暖的，对坐在阴凉处的人来说却是凉爽的。有些是由于个人的精神成分造成的：例如，虽然季节性情感障碍通常被理解为在黑暗、寒冷的冬季月份的抑郁经历，但对于某些人来说，夏季会导致季节性抑郁。再说一次，并不是人们不喜欢佛蒙特州或太平洋西北部，也不是我想把余生都关在沙漠的地狱里。这只是试图确定哪种天气——实际上是气候——最有利于退休生活。与讨论哪些美国城市天气最好的各种消息来源一致，我看到了我之前美国人（尤其是退休人员）在高速公路上穿的小路。我曾在美国的许多地方生活过，从东北到西南。我知道我每天在户外度过几个小时，或者至少在窗户打开的情况下需要什么样的天气。如果我必须选择一个地方居住，在美国，西南地区，或者至少在南方的某个地方，将是最安全的选择。我确实认为，如果有选择，大多数人会更喜欢一些季节性变化。温暖到温和的冬天可能没问题——甚至可能是理想的——只要它不干扰能够轻松到户外并长时间呆在外面的优先事项。
从我上小学起，我就看到了显示世界各地不同气候区的地图，例如维基百科的这个例子，显然取自美国国家海洋和大气管理局 (NOAA)：现在事实证明，气候地图产品具有自从我的小学时代以来有所改善。我的搜索导致了许多有趣的交互式（例如，可缩放）地图。在我检查过的那些中，MeteoEarth 提供了一个非常直观的地球仪，可以选择放大（滚动鼠标滚轮）、四处移动（左键单击并拖动）以及查看温度、降水、云量、风和其他的任意组合元素，现在和接下来的 24 小时（以便查看隔夜变化）。 《国家地理》还提供了一个地球仪，可以更好地查看气候带和国界的重叠情况，并可以选择不同的底图（例如，地形图、卫星照片）；目前，它的其他功能似乎不发达或功能失调。 NOAA 提供了一张不可缩放的交互式地图，但它确实允许我点击特定的气候带以获取有关该类型区域的一段通用信息（例如，“温和到温暖的夏季和寒冷的冬季”；“内陆地区夏季也可能有更多的降水”）。似乎人们喜欢美国南部或西部的天气可以用气候类型来概括。维基百科解释说，有不同的气候分类系统——其中，柯本系统是最常用的。世界气象组织 (WMO) 解释了计量学中使用的几种不同类型的平均值，并指出我可以在几个世界数据中心中进行选择。有了这些背景信息，我又回到了美国南部和西南部人们喜欢什么样的气候的问题上，仔细检查交互式和常规地图，显示 Köppen 气候区，告诉我实际上可能有多个气候区在美国南部和西南部。同一个都市区。从我所见，似乎最受欢迎的柯本气候类型包括 Cfa（例如，在美国东南部 38°N 100°W）、Csa（例如，萨克拉门托、阿纳海姆）、Csb（例如，旧金山、长滩）、BSk（例如，阿尔伯克基、尔湾、圣地亚哥沿海）和 BWh（例如，凤凰城）。我想知道这些是否也是国外首选的气候类型。在全球范围内，11 个不同的来源（即《今日美国》、TMW、赫芬顿邮报、旅行者、Weatherwise、GoTopTens、TripsToDiscover、AntonioGarzon、Earth & World、Tourist Maker、Mother Nature Network）列出了据称天气完美的城市。在这些来源命名的众多城市中，至少有三个来源同意这些城市，按降序排列：圣地亚哥，加利福尼亚；拉斯帕尔马斯（在大加那利群岛）；马拉加（或太阳海岸），西班牙；澳大利亚悉尼（或阿德莱德）；巴西圣保罗（或里约热内卢）；檀香山（或其他夏威夷）；法国尼斯（或马赛）；洛哈，厄瓜多尔；中国昆明；南非伊丽莎白港（或德班或开普敦）；和哥伦比亚麦德林。还提到了圣地亚哥以北、欧申赛德和圣路易斯奥比斯波之间的几个沿海城市。我选择了上一段中的长滩来代表他们，所以从这个意义上说，长滩也被其中几位旅行作家所牵连。我不确定查找这些地方的柯本气候代码的最佳方法是什么。我在 Quora 上发布了一个问题，询问是否有提供该信息的列表或电子表格。这个问题仍然没有答案。最终我自己回答了。我给出的答案是，我在 400 Cities Digital Nomad Index 中找到了 SG Kinsmann 提供的可下载电子表格，其中提供了 373 个城市的 Köppen 代码。为了检查该电子表格，我手动查找了上一段中列出的地点的气候类型。为此，我一开始想使用地图，但可用的地图看起来并不十分具体，而且似乎需要在不熟悉的地方及其周围的几种气候类型之间进行判断。我的查找结果和我使用的来源如下： 此列表中的气候代码包含了我在美国西南部热门城市（上图）中确定的所有代码。该列表表明消息来源不同意，就像我已经检查过的美国城市（上图）。岛上的混乱似乎更严重。加那利群岛显然有许多不同的气候，主要是 BWh，但也包括 BSh 和 Csb 等，夏威夷杂志（Paiva，2015）报道夏威夷有十种不同的气候。
无论如何，星号 (*) 表示在 Kinsmann 电子表格中为上述列出的城市指定了哪个气候代码。金斯曼与上述所有来源的不同之处在于至少部分得到了 Plantmaps 的支持，给圣地亚哥一个 Csa，给檀香山一个 Aw。金斯曼还给麦德林提供了一个 Cfb——根据维基百科的说法是合理的——并且没有拉斯帕尔马斯、尼斯、洛哈或昆明的数据。我得出的结论是，金斯曼的电子表格有些不完整，与上述来源不是高度一致，但它并没有不恰当的不同，因此可能足以满足一般用途。 Köppen 系统中有 29 个代码。上面我将称之为世界各地“完美天气城市”的清单，包括理想的美国城市的例子，包含了这 29 个城市中的 11 个（即 Af、Am、As、BSh、BSk、BWh、Cfa、Cfb、 Csa、Csb、Cwb）。虽然并非所有这 11 个都明确适用于完美的天气城市，但它们似乎足够接近，具有潜在的应用价值。同时，出于目前的目的，必须缩短 29 的列表，因为许多其他 Köppen 代码仅适用于与此处无关的极端天气区域。换句话说，这里确定的代码可能占地球整个部分使用的代码的一半之多，人们甚至可以想象在那里找到好天气。因此，似乎对气候代码的这种研究不一定有多大帮助，以努力寻找最佳天气地点。此外，虽然一些此类代码仅适用于较小的区域，但其他代码则相当广泛。例如，在北美，Cfa 类型覆盖了从德克萨斯州圣安东尼奥到肯塔基州列克星敦到佛罗里达州南部的大部分地区，BSk 气候区从墨西哥中部延伸到加拿大。 BWh 代码可能覆盖了非洲的三分之一，至少覆盖了澳大利亚的大部分地区。由于这些原因，理想天气的区域似乎不仅受气候类型的限制，而且还受温度和其他天气因素的限制。许多网站都提供享有美国最佳天气的城市列表。例如，AreaVibes、MarketWatch、AARP、US News、Suntan.com 和 CurrentResults。其中一些名单特别面向退休人员，这很有趣：可能是退休人员可以根据天气更自由地选择他们的位置，或者可能是天气对许多退休人员来说是最引人注目的事情。或两者。其中一些列表得出了奇怪的结论。例如：AreaVibes 建议德克萨斯州科珀斯克里斯蒂市是美国十大天气最佳城市之一。为了支持这一观点，AreaVibes 说：“夏季的平均气温在 80 年代左右。”该文章链接到一个单独的网页，澄清了 84F 的 8 月平均值是由白天最高可达 104F 的高点得出的。借助 Outflux 工具或许可以更好地检查这些数据，该工具报告说，在大多数月份中，科珀斯克里斯蒂市不仅比新奥尔良更热，而且风也更大，更潮湿——而且这两个城市都在 FarmersAlmanac 最差城市名单上夏季天气。 CurrentResults 指出，在它看来，西弗吉尼亚州的查尔斯顿是天气最好的州的亚军，其气候使其成为一个宜居的地方。但 Outflux 表明，查尔斯顿西弗吉尼亚州冬季平均低温低于 32 华氏度，降水量比西雅图多，在一年中的大部分月份，多云天数几乎相同。 MarketWatch 将内华达州卡森城评为全美气候最佳的城市之一。但是诺顿得出了一个非常不同的结论。当平均温度在 55F 到 75F 之间，最低和最高温度在 45F 到 85F 之间，并且没有明显的降水或积雪深度时，诺顿根据每年的天数对地点进行评级。按照这些标准，卡森城位于或接近该国天气最糟糕的地区之一。顺便提一下，截至 2014 年，由于气候变化，卡森城比美国任何其他城市都经历了更极端的气温上升
最佳天气城市名单的制定者确实倾向于就类似地点的简短名单达成一致，特别是包括南加州（例如圣地亚哥、圣路易斯奥比斯波）和佛罗里达州（例如基韦斯特、萨拉索塔、坦帕）的城市），偶尔会得到新墨西哥州（特别是陶斯、拉斯克鲁塞斯和圣达菲）和俄勒冈州（特别是本德和梅德福）以及其他几乎全部位于南部和西部的支持（例如，亚利桑那州普雷斯科特；Sequim ，华盛顿州；查尔斯湖，洛杉矶；乔治亚州圣玛丽斯；北卡罗来纳州阿什维尔；科罗拉多州大章克申；犹他州圣乔治；德克萨斯州沃思堡）。为了深入了解温度，我回到了本文前面引用的搜索。在那里，我发现了一些有趣的研究报告：Steiger 等人。 (2016) 发现，在德国南部阿尔卑斯山为期 7 天的夏季旅行中，受访者报告的理想温度范围为 70-77F，而海滩游客更喜欢 77-90F 的范围。虽然年龄较大或从事体育活动（例如，徒步旅行）的阿尔卑斯山游客更喜欢这个 (70-77F) 范围内的凉爽温度，但有孩子的家庭更喜欢温暖的温度。爱德华兹等人。 （2015 年）发现，从 3 岁开始，学习时间超过 5 年的儿童最有可能在 65F 左右的温度下活跃。 65 华氏度以上每升高 10 度，他们的中度到剧烈的体力活动每天就会减少 5 分钟。 65F 以下每降低 10 度，此类活动就会减少 17 分钟。降水和风速的增加也导致此类活动减少。 Hewer 等人。 （ 2016 年）发现，在公园游客中，当最高气温在旺季超过 91 华氏度（平季为 84 华氏度）时，以及当温度降至 52 华氏度以下时，游客人数下降。他们建议海滩游客可能特别受日温度的影响，而公园游客可能更受多日平均值的影响。 Zivin 和 Neidell (2014) 在对美国时间使用调查中 15 岁或以上美国人的数据进行分析时发现，个人平均每天进行约 44 分钟的户外休闲活动。在最高气温为 25 华氏度的日子里，他们平均比在最高气温为 76 华氏度或更高的日子里少 37 分钟的休闲时间。当最高室外温度超过 95F 时，每日室内休闲活动急剧增加。由于最高温度超过 85 华氏度，高暴露于气候的行业的工人分配的劳动时间较少。在 100 华氏度时，他们每天分配给劳动力的时间比最大值为 76-80 华氏度时少了大约一小时。曾经……的人
note_en:
I was thinking about becoming an expatriate (“expat”) — about living abroad, at least for a while. The question was, where? I had already  done some work to figure out which places were most affordable; now I wanted to know where I could find the best weather.
 (Note: in the process of developing this post, I concluded that weather would be only one factor affecting my planning, and not necessarily the most important one. Thus, I did not finish fully exploring weather to the extent originally anticipated. As such, the best use of this analysis may be to identify criteria of interest when considering the weather of specific places. In a few spots, this incomplete writeup may also have minor problems of coherence.)
  In trying to find the world’s best weather, there were some things I had to sort out. One was that you can have it all. You can have four seasons with a cold winter, and you can also have nice, warm, sunny January weather. You just can’t have it in the same place. You have to get in your vehicle and drive, or climb on the plane and fly. To enjoy this flexibility, it helps to live in a geographically and climatically diverse country like the U.S. — or (judging from  Google Earth) Argentina, or perhaps Italy; or in a place where the mountains rise up out of the sea to put those cold and warm spots right next to each other, like the west coast of South America.
 But I wasn’t planning on being a snowbird — spending winter months in a warmer location, that is, and then going somewhere else in the summer months. Although I wasn’t sure what the research said, I had heard that people who are gone for a substantial chunk of the year may find themselves socializing primarily with other snowbirds, if any are available and interested in socializing; they may not engage very deeply with society and community in the places they live. Snowbirding can also be difficult for those who want the advantages of a resident’s visa in their target countries: apparently there could be legal limits on how long one can be gone, at least in the early years. Of course, there can also be travel expenses in shlepping back and forth between locations, as well as the higher costs of maintaining two properties, keeping full sets of possessions in two places, and/or being limited to short leases.
 The mission, here, was to identify a destination where I would want to stay. Not that I wouldn’t consider going somewhere else sometime; I just wanted to make my first choice the best one. Which raised the question: in weather, what is “best”?
 I decided there was a good answer to that question — that it wasn’t necessarily just a matter of opinion — and that it mattered. Lucas and Lawless ( 2013, misconstrued by Darack,  2014;  accord Feddersen et al.,  2013) found that weather does not make a difference in the average person’s degree of life satisfaction. But that might not apply to people for whom daily exposure to the outdoors is an important element of preferred lifestyle, or whose social interaction is affected by weather conditions (e.g., Perry,  2014) — including particularly snowbirds (Simpson & Siguaw,  2012). Considerable research has demonstrated that exposure to the outdoors tends to have a positive impact upon psychological well-being (e.g., Bratman et al.,  2015; Buchecker & Degenhardt,  2015; Whear et al.,  2014; see also Dolan & Laffan,  2016). So if good weather got a person outside more frequently, then maybe the pursuit of good weather would help. Along these lines,  a search led to several additional interesting articles:
 In a study of office workers in China, consistent with other research, Cai ( 2013) found that productivity and satisfaction with the location of one’s workstation generally increased with proximity to windows (but declined for those sitting at desks immediately next to windows) and were higher on east, south, and west (i.e., at least partly sunny) sides of buildings than on the north side.
 Glimcher and Tymula ( 2015) cited research finding that older people are more vulnerable to weather and climate changes; that people are more risk-averse on cloudy days, when preceding days were not cloudy; that luminance (i.e., intensity and duration of sunshine) promotes optimism but also inconsistency, especially when luminance is higher than on previous days; and that bad mood (i.e., pessimism) improves analytical performance and memory (suggesting improved consistency).
 Chang (c.  2016) alluded to (but failed to specifically cite) research suggesting that time spent outdoors improves health in several ways: reduced stress and depression levels; improved creativity and productivity; improved ability to concentrate; Vitamin D absorption; for older people, improved mobility and ability to participate in daily activities, with reduced complaints of aches and of sleep problems; improved social skills for older dementia and stroke patients; strengthened immune systems; and improved eye health, quality of sleep, and self-esteem. ASC ( n.d.) echoed those points and added (with citations) that indoor air tends to be significantly more polluted than outdoor air, and that outdoor activity increases mobility, energy level, and happiness for people generally (i.e., not just older people).
 In short, these sources suggested that, as one might expect, exposure to the outdoors — particularly outdoors activity — is physically and mentally healthy. Therefore, I decided that the best weather was the weather that would most encourage a person to go outdoors and stay outdoors. For that purpose, key weather elements would be sunshine, warmth, and dryness — calling to mind the  Dreary Weather Index offered by Brian Brettschneider:
  Immigration from south of the border may have accounted for some of the  significant population growth in cities in Texas, Arizona, Florida, and southern California. But it did not account for population declines in Rust Belt cities from Milwaukee to Rochester. For a century at least, Americans had been flocking to retirement destinations in southern and southwestern states. While some internal migrants may take account of tax breaks, there was no comparable flow toward South Dakota or other  low-tax states in the north (see e.g., Conway & Rork,  2016, pp. 1012, 1021; Mazerov,  2014). Likewise, from what I had seen in my browsing, the preferred countries for expat retirement tended to be closer to the equator: Mexico, for example, and Thailand — not, for the most part, Canada or Sweden, despite their potential social, cultural, and/or linguistic advantages. American retirees willing to take a chance seemed much more inclined to consider affordable Portugal or Panama than affordable Estonia or Siberia.
 Granted, not everyone will enjoy the same climate, or the same weather events. Some of that is due to individual physical characteristics: metabolism and other aspects of a person’s body will influence how s/he reacts to warmer or cooler temperatures. Some is due to the conditions in which one encounters the weather. What is warm to a person doing physical work in the sunshine can be cool to a person sitting in the shade. Some is due to personal psychiatric composition: for example, while Seasonal Affective Disorder is commonly understood as the experience of depression during dark, cold winter months,  for some it is summertime that brings on seasonal depression.
 Again, it wasn’t that people don’t love Vermont or the Pacific Northwest, and it wasn’t that I would want to spend the rest of my days locked into a desert hellhole. This was just an attempt to identify the kind of weather — climate, actually — that would be most conducive to a tolerable retirement. Consistent with various  sources discussing which U.S. cities had the best weather, I saw the paths worn in the freeways by the Americans (and especially the retirees) before me. I had lived in a variety of places within the United States, from Northeast to Southwest. I knew what kind of weather it took for me to spend hours each day outdoors, or at least with the windows open. If I had to choose one place to live in, within the U.S., a place in the Southwest, or at least somewhere in the South, would be the safest bet.
 I did think that, given a choice, most people would prefer some seasonal variation. A warm to mild winter might be OK — might even be ideal — as long as it did not interfere with the priority of being able to go outdoors easily and stay out there for long stretches.
  Ever since my elementary school days, I had seen  maps showing different climate zones around the world, such as this example from  Wikipedia, apparently taken from the U.S. National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA):
  Now it turned out that the climate map offerings had improved since my grade-school days.  My search led to a number of interesting interactive (e.g., zoomable) maps. Among those I examined,  MeteoEarth offered a very intuitive globe, with options to zoom in (roll the mouse wheel), move around (left-click-drag), and view any combination of temperature, precipitation, cloud cover, wind, and other elements, now and for the next 24 hours (so as to view overnight changes).  National Geographic also offered a globe with better viewing of the overlap of climate zones and national boundaries, and with an option to choose different base maps (e.g., topographical, satellite photo); at present its other features seemed to be undeveloped or dysfunctional.  NOAA offered an interactive map that was not zoomable, but that did allow me to click on a specific climate zone for a paragraph’s worth of generic information about that type of zone (e.g., “mild to warm summers and cold winters”; “Inland locations may also have more precipitation during the summer months”).
 It seemed that what people liked about the weather in the American South or West could be summarized in terms of climate types.  Wikipedia explained that there were different climate classification systems — and that, of these, the Köppen system was the most commonly used. The World Meterological Organization ( WMO) explained several different kinds of  averages used in meterology and pointed me toward a choice among several  world data centers.
 Armed with that background information, I returned to the question of what kind of climate people liked, in the southern and southwestern U.S. Closer examination of  interactive and  regular maps, displaying Köppen climate zones, instructed me that there could actually be multiple climate zones within the same metropolitan area. From what I saw, it seemed the most popular Köppen climate types would include Cfa (found in, for instance, the U.S. southeast of 38°N 100°W), Csa (e.g., Sacramento, Anaheim), Csb (e.g., San Francisco, Long Beach), BSk (e.g., Albuquerque, Irvine, coastal San Diego), and BWh (e.g., Phoenix).
 I wondered whether those were also the preferred climate types abroad. Worldwide, 11 different sources (i.e.,   USA Today,  TMW,   Huffington Post,  Traveller,  Weatherwise,  GoTopTens,  TripsToDiscover,  AntonioGarzon,  Earth & World,  Tourist Maker,  Mother Nature Network) listed cities with supposedly perfect weather. Among the many cities named by such sources, there was agreement by at least three sources on these cities, in descending order: San Diego, CA; Las Palmas (in the Grand Canary Islands); Malaga (or the Costa del Sol), Spain; Sydney (or Adelaide), Australia; Sao Paulo (or Rio de Janeiro), Brazil; Honolulu (or other Hawaii); Nice (or Marseille), France; Loja, Ecuador; Kunming, China; Port Elizabeth (or Durban or Cape Town), South Africa; and Medellin, Colombia. Several coastal cities north of San Diego, between Oceanside and San Luis Obispo, were also mentioned. I chose Long Beach, from the previous paragraph, to represent them, so in that sense Long Beach was also implicated by several of these travel writers.
 I was not sure what was the best way to look up the Köppen climate codes for those places. I  posted a question on Quora, asking if there was a list or spreadsheet offering that information. That question remained unanswered; eventually I answered it myself. The answer I gave was that I had found  a downloadable spreadsheet offered by  SG Kinsmann at  the 400 Cities Digital Nomad Index, providing the Köppen codes for 373 cities. To check that spreadsheet, I manually looked up the climate types for the places listed in the preceding paragraph. To do that, I thought at first of using  a map, but the available maps did not look very specific and would seem to require judgment calls among several climate types, in and around unfamiliar places. The results of my lookups, and the sources I used, were as follows:
  The climate codes in this list captured all of the codes that I had identified in popular southwestern American cities (above). The list demonstrates that sources disagreed, as in the U.S. cities I had already checked (above). The confusion seemed to be worse on islands. The Canary Islands apparently had a number of different climates, mostly BWh but also including BSh and Csb among others, and  Hawai’i magazine (Paiva,  2015) reported that Hawaii had ten different kinds of climate.
 At any rate, asterisks (*) indicate which climate code was designated, in the Kinsmann spreadsheet, for the foregoing listed cities. Kinsmann differed from all of the foregoing sources in ways that were at least partly supported by  Plantmaps, in giving San Diego a Csa, and giving Honolulu an Aw. Kinsmann also gave Medellin a Cfb — reasonably, according to  Wikipedia — and had no data for Las Palmas, Nice, Loja, or Kunming. I concluded that Kinsmann’s spreadsheet was somewhat incomplete, and was not highly consistent with the foregoing sources, but that it did not differ ineptly, and therefore would probably be adequate for general purposes.
 There were 29 codes in  the Köppen system. The foregoing list of what I will call “perfect weather cities” from around the world, including the examples of desirable U.S. cities, contained 11 of those 29 (i.e., Af, Am, As, BSh, BSk, BWh, Cfa, Cfb, Csa, Csb, Cwb). While not all of those 11 clearly applied to the perfect weather cities, it appeared they were close enough to have potential application. Meanwhile, the list of 29 would have to be shortened for present purposes, as a number of other Köppen codes applied only to extreme weather areas that would not be relevant here. In other words, the codes identified here could account for as much as half of the codes used throughout the entire portion of the planet where one would even imagine finding good weather. Thus, it appeared that this look into climate codes was not necessarily helping much, in the effort to home in on the best weather locations.
 Moreover, while some such codes applied only to smallish areas, others were quite expansive. For instance, in North America, the Cfa type covered a zone from San Antonio TX to Lexington KY to much of southern Florida, and the BSk climate region stretched from central Mexico to Canada. The BWh code covered perhaps a third of Africa and at least that much of Australia. For such reasons, it appeared that the zone of ideal weather would be limited, not merely by climate type, but also by temperature and other weather elements.
 Many websites offer lists of cities that enjoy what is supposedly the best weather in the U.S. Examples include  AreaVibes,  MarketWatch,  AARP,   US News,  Suntan.com, and  CurrentResults. Several of those lists are particularly oriented toward retirees, and that is interesting: it could be that retirees have more freedom to choose their location according to weather, or it could be that weather is the most compelling thing for many retirees. Or both. Some of those lists arrive at strange conclusions. For example:
 AreaVibes suggests that Corpus Christi, TX is one of the ten best weather cities in the America. In support of this view, AreaVibes says, “Average temperatures in the summer hang around the 80s.” That writeup links to  a separate webpage clarifying that the August average of 84F results from daytime highs ranging up to 104F. These data are perhaps better examined with the aid of  the Outflux tool, which reports that, in most months, Corpus Christi is not only hotter but also windier and more humid than New Orleans — and both are on  the FarmersAlmanac list of cities with the worst summer weather.
 CurrentResults states that, in its view, Charleston, West Virginia is a runner-up for states with best weather, with a climate that make it a good place to live. But  Outflux indicates that Charleston WV has wintertime average low temperatures below 32F, with more precipitation than Seattle, in most months of the year, and nearly as many cloudy days.
 MarketWatch identifies Carson City, NV as one of the nation’s best weather cities. But  Norton arrives at a very different conclusion. Norton rates locations according to the number of days per year when the mean temperature is between 55F and 75F, the minimum and maximum temperatures are between 45F and 85F, and there is no significant precipitation or snow depth. By those standards, Carson City is in, or close to, one of the worst weather areas in the country. Incidentally, as of 2014, Carson City had experienced  a more extreme rise of temperature due to climate change than any other city in the U.S.
 The makers of the lists of best-weather cities do tend to agree on a short list of similar locations, notably including cities in southern California (e.g., San Diego, San Luis Obispo), and Florida (e.g., Key West, Sarasota, Tampa), with occasional endorsements of New Mexico (esp. Taos, Las Cruces, and Santa Fe) and Oregon (esp. Bend and Medford), as well as others, almost entirely in the South and West (e.g., Prescott, AZ; Sequim, WA; Lake Charles, LA; St. Marys, GA; Asheville, NC; Grand Junction, CO; St. George, UT; Fort Worth, TX).
  For insights into temperature, I returned to  the search cited earlier in this post. There, I discovered a number of interesting research reports:
 Steiger et al. ( 2016) found that, during a seven-day summer trip to the Alps in southern Germany, survey respondents reported an ideal temperature range of 70-77F, whereas beachgoers preferred the range of 77-90F. While Alps tourists who were older or who engaged in sporting activities (e.g., hiking) preferred cooler temperatures within this (70-77F) range, families with children preferred warmer temperatures.
 Edwards et al. ( 2015) found that children studied over a period of five years, starting at age 3, were most likely to be active at temperatures around 65F. For every 10 degrees above 65F, their moderate to vigorous physical activity was reduced by five minutes per day. For every 10 degrees below 65F, such activity was reduced by 17 minutes. Increasing precipitation and wind speed also yielded reductions in such activity.
 Hewer et al. ( 2016) found that, among park visitors, visitation declined when maximum temperatures exceeded 91F in peak seasons (84F in shoulder seasons), and also when temperatures dropped below 52F. They suggested that beach tourists may be influenced especially by daily temperature, while park visitors may be more influenced by multiday averages.
 In an analysis of data taken from Americans aged 15 or older in the American Time Use Survey, Zivin and Neidell ( 2014) found that individuals engaged in an average of about 44 minutes of outdoor leisure per day. They averaged 37 fewer minutes of such leisure on days where the maximum temperature was 25F than on days where the maximum was 76F or higher. Daily indoor leisure increased sharply when maximum outdoor temperatures exceeded 95F. Workers in industries with high exposure to climate allocated less time to labor as maximum temperatures exceeded 85F. At 100F, they allocated about one hour less to labor per day than when the maximum was 76-80F. People who wer
......
title: 世界上最好的天气在哪里？ (2020)
title_en: Where Is the World’s Best Weather? (2020)

蒲团推销员杰森·帕吉特 (Jason Padgett) 除了参加派对和追女孩外，对任何事情都不关心，然后一个决定性的夜晚永远改变了他。 BBC Future 为您带来了深入而严谨的故事，以帮助您应对当前的流行病，但我们知道您想阅读的不仅仅是这些。所以现在我们专门推出一个系列来帮助你逃脱。我们将在 Lockdown Longreads 中重新审视过去三年中最受欢迎的功能。从关于世界上最伟大的太空任务的故事到关于我们的猫是否真的爱我们的真相，将非法渔民绳之以法的史诗般的狩猎，以及让埋藏已久的第二次世界大战坦克复活的小团队，您将找到一切.你不会找到的是任何参考，嗯，你知道什么。享受。杰森·帕吉特 (Jason Padgett) 随处可见数学。即使像刷牙这样普通的事情也受数学支配——他打开水龙头，将牙刷浸入水中 16 次。 “我不知道为什么我喜欢完美的正方形，”他说。 “这不仅仅是一个完美的正方形，它是 2 的 4 或 4 次方，但我只是喜欢完美的正方形......我会自动对所有东西做那些事。” Padgett 痴迷于数学，理解如此复杂的概念，被称为天才。他当然具有手工绘制重复几何图案（称为分形）的罕见天赋。但这位来自阿拉斯加的前蒲团推销员并不总是对数字有办法。就在 17 年前，他在华盛顿州塔科马过着截然不同的生活。
“我很肤浅，”他笑着说。 “生活围绕着女孩旋转，聚会、喝酒、宿醉醒来，然后出去追逐女孩，然后又去酒吧。” “我曾经说过‘数学很愚蠢，你怎么能在现实世界中使用它’？我认为这是一个聪明的声明。我真的相信了。”但在 2002 年 9 月 13 日星期五晚上，一切都变了。 （阅读更多关于为什么有些人会突然成为天才的信息）。在与朋友外出时，帕吉特在卡拉 OK 酒吧外遭到两名男子的袭击和抢劫。他们拿走了他已经破烂的皮夹克。他回忆说：“当第一个跑到我身后用力砸我的后脑勺时，我听到和感觉到这种低沉的砰砰声。” “我看到了这股白光，就像有人拍照一样。接下来我知道我跪在地上，一切都在旋转，我不知道我在哪里，也不知道我是如何到达那里的。” Padgett 摇摇晃晃地去了街对面的一家医院，在那里他被告知他有脑震荡，并且由于肠道一拳而导致肾脏流血。 “他们给了我一针止痛药，然后送我回家，”他回忆道。但是回到家后，帕吉特的行为迅速而戏剧性地发生了变化。他遭受了创伤性脑损伤，这会导致强迫症 - 强迫症。以杰森为例，他越来越害怕外面的世界，只会出门囤积食物。
“我只记得在房子的所有窗户上钉了毯子和毛巾……我记得实际上使用了这种喷雾泡沫并将前门粘上。”强迫症让帕吉特不合理地害怕细菌，这对他的女儿产生了连锁反应，女儿会在与前伴侣的监护权谈判中和他在一起。 “当她过来时，我会痴迷地洗手和清洁，”他说。 “我想做的第一件事就是给她脱鞋，换上干净的衣服，洗手。”但是，当帕吉特因他的袭击而经历所有这些负面后果时，一些令人难以置信的事情也在发生。杰森看待事物的方式发生了变化。 “所有弯曲的东西看起来都有些像素化，”他解释道。 “从下水道流下来的水看起来不再是光滑、流动的东西，它看起来像这些小切线。”云也发生了同样的事情，阳光在树木和水坑之间流动。对帕吉特来说，这个世界本质上就像一个复古的电子游戏。看到他周围环境的这种截然不同的看法在帕吉特身上引起了矛盾的情绪。 “我很惊讶……很困惑。它很美，但同时也很可怕。”由于这些愿景，帕吉特开始思考与数学和物理有关的巨大问题。考虑到他当时像隐士一样的生活，当他在网上广泛阅读数学时，互联网成为了他宝贵的信息来源。
他偶然发现了一个关于分形的网页，这引起了他的共鸣。这是一个困难的数学概念，从最基本的角度来说，它可以比作雪花。当您放大时，您会看到它由连接在一起的较小雪花组成，再次放大，这些雪花由较小的雪花组成，依此类推，直到无穷大。 Padgett 对这个概念很着迷，但直到有一天他的女儿问他电视是如何工作的，他才用语言来形容它。 “当你看着电视屏幕时，你看到一个圆圈，它真的不是一个圆圈，”他说。 “它是由长方形或正方形制成的，如果你仔细观察，圆的边缘实际上是一个锯齿形。你可以把这些像素切成两半，再把它们切成两半，你会越来越接近一个完美的圆，但你永远不会真正达到一个，因为你可以永远把像素切成两半，所以分辨率会变得更好，但你永远不会有一个完美的圆。” Padgett 觉得有必要进一步探索这个有趣的概念。于是，他开始画画。而且他一直在画。 “我实际上有一千张或更多的圆形、分形图，以及我能画的每一种形状。这是我能够有效地传达我所看到的东西的唯一方式。” Padgett 相信他的画“掌握着通向宇宙的钥匙”并且非常重要，以至于他需要随身携带它们。有一天，在一次罕见的外出旅行中，一个人找到了他，他注意到帕吉特的画，并告诉他这些画看起来很数学。
“我试图根据普朗克长度（物理学家马克斯普朗克开发的一种微小测量单位）和量子黑洞来描述时空的离散结构，”帕吉特告诉他。原来这个人是个物理学家，认出了帕吉特画的高级数学。他敦促他上数学课，这让帕吉特进入了社区大学，在那里他开始学习描述他的痴迷所需的语言。在像隐士一样生活了三年半之后，上学改变了帕吉特的一切。他开始为他的强迫症寻求心理帮助，甚至遇到了后来成为他妻子的女人。但为什么他会以如此奇怪和不同的方式看待事物？为什么他的世界现在由几何形状和图形组成？诗意地，是电视再次为他提供了线索。 Padgett 看到一个人，一个所谓的学者，他拥有非凡的数字能力，并谈论了他对数字的看法。 “我总是说数学是形状而不是数字，那是我第一次听到除了我以外的任何人谈论数字的样子，”Padgett 说。他在互联网上搜索更多信息，并遇到了现在在迈阿密大学工作的认知神经科学家 Berit Brogaard。两人在电话里聊了几个小时，从这些谈话中，布罗加德假设帕吉特有联觉——本质上是大脑的交叉连接，其中感官混杂在一起。 （了解更多关于联觉的信息——以及它是否可以学习）。据估计，它仅影响约 4% 的人口。一些联觉者在听到音乐时可能会看到某些颜色，或者在感受到某种特定情绪时会闻到一些不存在的东西。
这种情况是由其他人不存在的大脑部分之间的连接引起的。你可能是这样出生的，也可能是某种类型的创伤、损伤、中风、过敏反应，都可以改变大脑。 Brogaard 认为 Padgett 遭受​​的脑损伤导致他发展出一种联觉形式，在这种情况下，某些事物会在他的脑海中或投射在他的面前引发数学公式或几何形状的幻觉。她还假设通感使帕吉特成为后天学者。 “我们大多数人没有那种洞察力，因为我们没有将数学公式形象化，”布罗加德说。为了验证这些想法，布罗加德将帕吉特带到赫尔辛基阿尔托大学的大脑研究中心，在那里他接受了一系列大脑扫描。在核磁共振扫描仪中，数百个方程，包括假方程，在帕吉特眼前的屏幕上闪烁。研究人员随后观察了他大脑的哪些部分发出了反应。 Padgett 说：“他们发现我可以接触到我们无法有意识地接触到的大脑部分，而且视觉皮层正在与大脑中负责数学的部分协同工作，这显然是有道理的。” Brogaard 的假设被证明是正确的。 Padgett 被正式诊断出患有获得性学者综合征和一种联觉。终于，他有了答案。
自从他确诊后，Padgett 出版了一本关于他的经历的书，名为 Struck by Genius，他周游世界，向人们讲述他的故事并教育他们有关数学的知识。他的目标是通过出版或制作电影来帮助那些拥有独特或罕见/有趣生活的人。他甚至出售他的分形图。尽管帕吉特确认了他们并提出指控，但在那个决定性的九月晚上袭击他的两个人从未被定罪。然而，多年后，其中一名男子布雷迪·西蒙斯 (Brady Simmons) 写信给帕吉特道歉，当时他因自杀未遂而接受处方药成瘾治疗。从某种意义上说，袭击发生后的几年里，两个人的生活发生了变化。 “我是一个完全不同的人，”西蒙斯说。 “当我回顾过去那个深不可测的人时，我只是不明白我是如何存在于那个层次上的。” “我到处都看到它[美]，”他说。他被大多数人甚至没有注意到的简单事物如落在水坑上的雨滴迷住了。通过帕吉特的眼睛，水坑变成了复杂的涟漪图案，重叠形成星星或雪花的形状。他希望其他人都能看到他所看到的。他说：“在现实甚至存在的任何时候，你都应该在绝对的惊奇中四处走动。” “我正在经历这种数学觉醒，我们周围的一切都是绝对的魔法，或者你可以接近魔法。”

note_en:
Futon salesman Jason Padgett cared little about anything beyond partying and chasing girls, then one fateful night changed him forever.
 BBC Future has brought you in-depth and rigorous stories to help you navigate the  current pandemic, but we know that’s not all you want to read. So now we’re dedicating a series to help you escape. We’ll be revisiting our most popular features from the last three years in our   Lockdown Longreads .
 You’ll find everything from the story about the  world’s greatest space mission to the truth about whether  our cats really love us, the epic hunt to bring  illegal fishermen to justice and the small team which brings  long-buried World War Two tanks back to life. What you won’t find is any reference to, well, you-know-what. Enjoy.
 Jason Padgett sees maths everywhere. Even something as ordinary as brushing his teeth is governed by mathematics – he turns the tap on and dips his toothbrush into the water 16 times.
 “I don’t know why I like perfect squares,” he says. “It’s not just a perfect square, it’s two to the power of four or four squared but I just like perfect squares… I automatically do that stuff with everything.”
 Padgett is so obsessed with maths and understands such complex concepts, he's been called a genius. He certainly has a rare talent for drawing repeating geometric patterns – known as fractals – by hand.
  But the former futon salesman from Alaska hasn’t always had a way with numbers. Just under 17 years ago he was living a very different life in Tacoma, Washington.
 “I was very shallow,” he laughs. “Life rotated around girls, partying, drinking, waking up with a hangover and then going out and chasing girls and going out to bars again.”
  “I used to say ‘math is stupid, how can you use that in the real world’? And I thought that was like a smart statement. I really believed it.”
 But on the night of Friday 13 September 2002 everything changed.  (Read more about  why some people become sudden geniuses).
 While out with friends, Padgett was attacked and robbed by two men outside a karaoke bar. They took his already torn leather jacket.
  “I heard as much as felt this deep, low-pitched thud as the first guy ran up behind me and smashed me in the back of the head,” he recalls. “And I saw this puff of white light just like someone took a picture. The next thing I knew I was on my knees and everything was spinning and I didn’t know where I was or how I got there.”
 Padgett staggered to a hospital across the street where he was told he had concussion and a bleeding kidney thanks to a punch to the gut. “They gave me a shot of pain medication and sent me home,” he remembers.
 But once home, Padgett’s behaviour changed quickly and dramatically. He had sustained a traumatic brain injury, which can bring on obsessive compulsive disorder - OCD. In Jason's case, he became increasingly afraid of the outside world and would only leave his house to stock up on food.
 “I just remember nailing blankets and towels over all the windows in the house… I remember actually using this spray foam and gluing the front door shut.”
 The OCD had made Padgett irrationally afraid of germs, which had a knock-on effect on his daughter who would come to stay with him amidst custody negotiations with his ex-partner.
 “When she would come over I would obsessively wash my hands and clean,” he says. “The very first thing I would want to do is get her shoes off, get her into clean clothes, wash her hands.”
 But while Padgett was experiencing all these negative consequences from his attack, something incredible was happening too. The way Jason was seeing things changed.
  “Everything that was curved looked like it was slightly pixelated,” he explains. “Water coming down the drain didn’t look like it was a smooth, flowing thing anymore, it looked like these little tangent lines.”
 The same thing happened with clouds, sunlight streaming between trees and puddles. To Padgett, the world essentially looked like a retro video game. Seeing such a radically different view of his surroundings evoked conflicting emotions in Padgett. “I was surprised…confused. It was beautiful but it was also scary at the same time.”
 Because of these visions, Padgett began to think about huge questions in relation to mathematics and physics. Given his hermit-like existence at that time, the internet became a valuable source of information to him as he read extensively about mathematics online.
  He stumbled across a webpage about fractals which struck a chord with him. It’s a difficult mathematical concept which, put at its most basic, can be likened to a snowflake. When you zoom in, you will see it’s made up of smaller snowflakes connected together, zoom in again and those snowflakes are made of smaller snowflakes, and so on until infinity.
 Padgett was fascinated by this concept but didn’t yet have the words to describe it until one day his daughter asked him how the TV worked.
  “When you’re looking at a TV screen and you see a circle it’s really not a circle,” he says. “It’s made with rectangles or squares and, if you look close, the edge of the circle is really a zig zag. You can take those pixels and cut them in half and cut them in half and you get closer and closer to a perfect circle but you never actually reach one because you can keep cutting the pixels in half forever, so the resolution gets better but you never have a perfect circle.”
 Padgett felt compelled to explore this intriguing concept further. So, he began to draw. And he kept drawing.
  “I had literally a thousand or more drawings of circles, fractals, every shape that I could manage to draw. It was the only way I could manage to communicate effectively what I was seeing.”
 Padgett believed his drawings “held the key to the universe” and were so important that he needed to take them everywhere with him.
 While on a rare trip out one day, he was approached by a man who had noticed Padgett with his drawings and told him they looked mathematical.
  “I’m trying to describe the discrete structure of space time based on Planck length (a tiny unit of measurement developed by physicist Max Planck) and quantum black holes,” Padgett told him. It turned out the man was a physicist and recognised the high-level mathematics Padgett was drawing. He urged him to take a maths class, which led Padgett to enrol in a community college, where he began to learn the language he needed to describe his obsession.
 After three and a half years of living like a virtual hermit, going to school changed everything for Padgett. He started to get psychological help for his OCD and even met the woman who would become his wife.
 But why was he seeing things in such a strange and different way? Why was his world now comprised of geometric shapes and graphs?
 Poetically, it was television that again provided him with a clue. Padgett saw a man, a so-called savant, who had extraordinary numerical abilities and talked about what numbers looked like to him.
  “I would always describe that math was shapes not numbers and that was the first time I’d heard anybody but me talk about what numbers looked like,” says Padgett.
 He scoured the internet for more information and came across Berit Brogaard, a cognitive neuroscientist now at the University of Miami. The pair spent hours talking on the phone and from these conversations, Brogaard hypothesised that Padgett had synaesthesia – essentially a cross-wiring of the brain in which the senses get mixed up.  (Find out more about  synaesthesia — and whether it can be learnt).
  It is estimated to effect only around 4% of the population. Some synesthetes might see certain colours when they hear music or smell something that’s not there when feeling a particular emotion.
 The condition is caused by connections between parts of the brain that are not there in other people. You can be born this way or some type of trauma, an injury, a stroke, an allergic reaction, can change the brain.
 Brogaard believes the brain injury Padgett sustained caused him to develop a form of synaesthesia where certain things triggered visions of mathematical formulas or geometric shapes, either in his mind or projected in front of him. She also hypothesised that synaesthesia made Padgett an acquired savant.
 “Most of us don’t have that kind of insight because we don’t visualise mathematical formulas,” says Brogaard.
  To test these ideas, Brogaard brought Padgett to the Brain Research Unit of Aalto University in Helsinki, where he underwent a series of brain scans.
 While in the MRI scanner, hundreds of equations, including fake ones, flashed on a screen in front of Padgett’s eyes. The researchers then watched which parts of his brain lit up in response.
 “They found that I had access to parts of the brain that we don’t have conscious access to and also the visual cortex was working in conjunction with the part of the brain that does mathematics, which obviously makes sense,” says Padgett.
 Brogaard’s hypotheses turned out to be true. Padgett was formally diagnosed with acquired savant syndrome and a form of synaesthesia. Finally, he had answers.
 Since his diagnosis, Padgett has published a book about his experience called  Struck by Genius, he’s toured the world telling people his story and educating them about maths. He is aiming to help others who have had unique or rare/interesting lives by getting their stories published or made into movies. He even sells his drawings of fractals.
 The two men who attacked him that fateful September night were never convicted despite Padgett identifying them and pressing charges.
  Years later, however, one of the men, Brady Simmons, wrote to Padgett to apologise while he was undergoing treatment for prescription drug addiction following a suicide attempt. In a sense, two lives were changed in the years that followed the attack.
  “I’m a completely different person,” says Simmons. “When I look back the abysmal person that I was in the past, I just don’t see how I existed on that level.”
  “I see it [beauty] everywhere,” he says. He is mesmerised by simple things that most people don’t even notice such as raindrops falling on a puddle.
 Through Padgett’s eyes, the puddle is transformed into complex rippling patterns, overlapping and forming shapes like stars or snowflakes. And he wants everyone else to see what he sees.
 “You should be walking around in absolute amazement at all times that reality even exists,” he says. “I’m having this mathematical awakening and all around us is absolute magic or about as close as you can get to magic.”

note: 根据 NSO 在一项正在进行的诉讼中提交的特别法庭文件，Facebook 代表与有争议的监控供应商 NSO Group 接洽，试图购买一种可以帮助 Facebook 更好地监控其部分用户的工具。 Facebook 目前正在起诉 NSO，因为这家黑客公司如何利用 WhatsApp 中的漏洞帮助政府黑客入侵用户。 NSO 销售一种名为 Pegasus 的产品，它允许操作员远程感染手机并从中提取数据。根据 NSO 首席执行官 Shalev Hulio 的声明，两名 Facebook 代表于 2017 年 10 月与 NSO 接洽，要求购买 Pegasus 某些功能的使用权。当时，Facebook 正处于部署名为 Onavo Protect 的 VPN 产品的早期阶段，在某些用户不知情的情况下，该产品会分析下载该产品的用户的网络流量，以查看他们使用的其他应用程序。根据法庭文件，Facebook 的代表似乎对购买 Pegasus 的一部分作为远程入侵手机的黑客工具不感兴趣，而是作为一种更有效地监控已经安装了 Onavo 的用户的手机的一种方式。你为 NSO 或 Facebook 工作，还是曾经？我们很乐意听取您的意见。使用非工作电话或计算机，您可以通过 +44 20 8133 5190 的 Signal 安全联系 Joseph Cox，josephcox 的 Wickr，jfcox@jabber.ccc.de 的 OTR 聊天
note_en:
Facebook representatives approached  controversial surveillance vendor NSO Group to try and buy a tool that could help Facebook better monitor a subset of its users, according to an extraordinary court filing from NSO in an ongoing lawsuit.
  Facebook is currently suing NSO for how the hacking firm leveraged a vulnerability in WhatsApp to help governments hack users. NSO sells a product called Pegasus, which allows operators to remotely infect cell phones and lift data from them.
    According to  a declaration from NSO CEO Shalev Hulio, two Facebook representatives approached NSO in October 2017 and asked to purchase the right to use certain capabilities of Pegasus.
  At the time, Facebook was in the early stages of deploying a VPN product called Onavo Protect, which, unbeknownst to some users,  analyzed the web traffic of users who downloaded it to see what other apps they were using. According to the court documents, it seems the Facebook representatives were not interested in buying parts of Pegasus as a hacking tool to remotely break into phones, but more as a way to more effectively monitor phones of users who had already installed Onavo.
